evals.prompt: Write a blog post about technical debt. My audience is mid-level engineers who are frustrated that their managers don't take it seriously.
evals.expected_output: A full blog post in Sunny's voice: hook with quote or anecdote, unexpected analogy to reframe the problem, H2/H3 section structure, short punchy paragraphs, practical takeaway, 1-2 sentence conclusion.

assertions.name: has-h2-section-headers
assertions.description: Output uses H2 or H3 markdown headers to structure sections

assertions.name: has-conclusion-section
assertions.description: Output includes a Conclusion or In Summary section

assertions.name: avoids-generic-opener
assertions.description: Does NOT open with cliche phrases like 'In today's fast-paced world'

assertions.name: uses-analogy-or-framework
assertions.description: References at least one named analogy, framework, law, book, or real-world concept to reframe the argument
assertions.rubric: Does the output use a specific named concept, analogy, or framework (e.g. a named law, historical event, game mechanic, book title, research concept) to make the argument more memorable? Not just a generic comparison, but something with a proper name.

assertions.name: opinionated-direct-voice
assertions.description: The post takes a clear stance and uses direct, confident language rather than hedging
assertions.rubric: Does the post sound like it was written by someone with a strong point of view? It should make declarative claims, not hedge with 'some might argue' or 'it depends'. Look for phrases that own the perspective.

assertions.name: no-filler-bloat
assertions.description: Post is lean — no padding, no repeating the same point multiple ways
assertions.rubric: Does every paragraph advance the argument? Or does the post repeat points, add vague filler, or pad sections to seem more thorough? Score this pass if the post feels tight.

evals.prompt: Write a blog post about why remote work is actually good for engineering teams. I want to push back on the 'you have to be in the office to collaborate' narrative.
evals.expected_output: A blog post with a strong hook, clear contrary argument, an analogy or framework, section breakdown, practical conclusion. Should feel like Sunny wrote it — warm but direct, cites real things, short sections.

assertions.name: has-h2-section-headers
assertions.description: Output uses H2 or H3 markdown headers to structure sections

assertions.name: has-conclusion-section
assertions.description: Output includes a Conclusion or In Summary section

assertions.name: addresses-counter-narrative
assertions.description: Explicitly engages with and refutes the 'must be in office to collaborate' argument
assertions.rubric: Does the post directly address and push back on the idea that in-person presence is required for collaboration? It should steelman the opposing view at least briefly, then dismantle it.

assertions.name: uses-analogy-or-framework
assertions.description: References at least one named analogy, framework, or real concept
assertions.rubric: Does the output use a specific named concept, analogy, or framework to make the argument more memorable? Not just generic comparisons.

assertions.name: opinionated-direct-voice
assertions.description: Takes a clear stance, confident language, no excessive hedging
assertions.rubric: Does the post sound like someone with a strong point of view? Should make declarative claims.

evals.prompt: Help me write a LinkedIn post about the one thing I wish I knew earlier in my career: that saying no is a skill, not a personality flaw.
evals.expected_output: A shorter-form piece suited to LinkedIn: punchy opener, clear argument, practical takeaway. Should match Sunny's voice — opinionated, no filler, ends strong.

assertions.name: appropriate-length-for-linkedin
assertions.description: Output is concise — suitable for LinkedIn (roughly 150–500 words)
assertions.rubric: Is this post appropriately sized for LinkedIn? It should be punchy and scannable, not a 10-minute read. A good LinkedIn post gets in, makes its point, and gets out.

assertions.name: avoids-generic-opener
assertions.description: Does NOT open with cliche phrases
assertions.pattern: ^(In today|I hope|As a developer|Whether you|Are you)

assertions.name: ends-with-strong-close
assertions.description: Ends with a punchy takeaway or call to reflection — not a weak sign-off
assertions.rubric: Does the post end memorably? It should leave the reader with something they'll remember — a sharp line, a question, or a decisive statement. Not 'Hope this helps!' or 'Let me know your thoughts in the comments.'

assertions.name: opinionated-direct-voice
assertions.description: Takes a clear stance, sounds like a real person with a point of view
assertions.rubric: Does this sound like a genuine person sharing a hard-won opinion, not a generic LinkedIn thought-leader? Should be direct and confident.
